retrieved_memories.content: To produce a field-usable checklist: 1. Extract repeat failure patterns from real events. 2. Structure each failure as: symptom, what changed first, 1st check, if normal then next check, stop point, verify after fix. 3. Provide a fill-in template and a styled example using the same skeleton. 4. Format for concise, copy-paste usability in notebooks or work orders.

To create an operational diagnostic flow: 1. Use explicit, measurable rules (e.g., '2 hands showing same bad case') to stop blaming individuals; 2. Apply consistency checks (workaround identity, failure timing, upstream first deviation) to flag system/process issues; 3. Log minimal field data (case ID, timestamp, source, handler, path, first deviation, workaround/exact-process flags, outcome) for rapid field use.

When diagnosing process issues with limited time and data, prioritize checking timestamp/order trends first, then compare bad vs good cases by one variable at a time, and finally use consistency checks across users and processes to decide whether to stop on system vs workflow vs input.

To create a field-usable checklist: 1. Identify the actual past failure (e.g., sensor drift). 2. Map it to the requested structure: symptom → what changed first → 1st check → if normal then next check → stop point → verify after fix. 3. Output a fill-in template with one worked example using that past failure. Use the same format for the template and the example: list the symptom, first change, 1st check, if normal then next check, stop point, and verify after fix; make the example look like a real field note with specific, concrete phrasing. (Original: 2026-04-29T09:53:36)

To distinguish between system and operator issues, check if the workaround is consistent across users, whether the issue appears when the process is followed exactly, and whether the same case fails in different hands — this helps avoid misattributing tool-forced workarounds to operator error.

retrieved_memories.content: User is an experienced exhaust gas treatment engineer working on marine scrubber systems for tugboats and coastal freight vessels. They focus on troubleshooting sensors, verifying pH/flow/stack temp readings, dosing systems, load tests, and emissions documentation. They frequently work with technicians on repairs and recalibrations when systems drift. They have practical, measurable success stories: one time they fixed a sulfur-removal test failure by replacing a drifting sensor and got the vessel to pass inspection; another time they completed emissions paperwork early to avoid missing a storm-related inspection window. They value practical, credible, plain-English descriptions that sound like real work and prefer to keep technical writing clear and unadorned. They now use a structured, measurable approach to evaluate new opportunities: they assess controllability of changes (new area, new manager, new commute, new housing), score key criteria (pay after taxes, housing, schedule, manager, routine loss, bad-fit risk) on a 1-to-5 plain scale, and separate job risk from location risk to make practical, data-driven decisions. They now apply a verification-first triage to field checks and dockside issues: they confirm if it’s a hard stop or nuisance alarm, verify logs against current readings, check fast movers first, use handheld cross-checks, and only replace parts after confirming the issue. They follow a strict late-stay rule: 'only stay late if you can verify the fix and prove the system is back into spec.' They recommend noting who has authority to clear the issue after verification passes — that often stalls resolution more than equipment issues. They use a proactive, risk-aware approach to administrative tasks: they prioritize completing time-sensitive, sign-off-dependent tasks before weather-sensitive field work; organize field jobs by access risk (ladders, dock access, open decks) rather than just by vessel or order; schedule the most weather-vulnerable physical work for the next decent window; use a dynamic trigger (act when access looks marginal and the forecast shows the window shrinking) instead of fixed '2–3 checks'; and offer to help convert actual lists into strict now/next window/hold lists with access and deadline risks separated. They now recommend a site-check triage broken down by the first failing component (lighting, backup power, access, equipment). If power is the weak point, prioritize HMI/data logging/chargers; if access is the weak point, prioritize outdoor/ladder/dock work. They recommend having a charged headlamp with spares, a handheld meter with known-good leads, a charged phone/camera, printed checklists and blank logs, calibration tools, rain gear/gloves in the car, and spare fuses/consumables. They add a key rule: for any check needing power to verify, ensure manual documentation can be done if the system drops mid-run — their last scrubber test under backup power was saved by manual paperwork when access later got ugly. They now offer to help convert the described failure-mode triage into a decision tree. They now add explicit measured tread depth and recorded tire movement to the paperwork. They will verify tire size, load index/speed rating, DOT date code, measured tread depth, rotation pattern, PSI (cold), TPMS reset/relearn, torque spec, and existing damage. They will take a quick photo of each tire before handing it over to prevent invoice/record errors. They now add mileage to the paperwork to timestamp tread wear and link it to rotation intervals. They now want a real pre-start checklist for field checks, not a broad safety outline. They want to verify what to do before leaving the shop. retrieved_memories.content: User prefers short, direct messages with no extra fluff when communicating. User wants the message to be clear and not overly wordy. [Original memory timestamp: 2026-04-29T11:16:05] User prefers direct, concise messaging that flags quote discrepancies and requests a detailed itemized breakdown. Specifically, wants to request a detailed breakdown of labor, parts, and diagnostic charges and clearly separate required from optional/recommended work. User is willing to send a firmer, more direct version of the message if the shop pushes back or if they want to be blunt. [Updated timestamp: 2026-04-29T11:24:00] User wants to avoid long back-and-forth conversations when raising quote mismatches. [New addition timestamp: 2026-04-29T11:24:00]

User wants a tight, field-usable checklist format with a specific structure: symptom, what changed first, 1st check, if normal then next check, stop point, verify after fix. This is a tight, fill-in template with a matching example using the same skeleton.
User’s prioritization order is: 1. Tasks that stall other work; 2. Tasks with fixed cutoffs; 3. Tasks needing prep before touching; 4. Tasks that can sit without cost.
User will provide a specific maintenance list to be sorted by safety, deadline, dependency, and fallback.

User prefers a minimal scratch-sheet format with fields: case ID, timestamp, source/input, handler, path/system, first deviation, workaround Y/N, exact-process Y/N, outcome, and optionally queue position or shift. (Original timestamp: 2026-04-29T08:55:04). Newer preference: prefers 8 fields: case ID, timestamp, source/input, handler, path/system, first deviation, workaround Y/N, exact-process Y/N. (Newer timestamp: 2026-04-29T08:55:34). User will add queue position or shift to the scratch-sheet only when timing looks suspect. (Newer timestamp: 2026-04-29T08:55:34).

stages.reason: Yes — discussing a repair quote with mismatched numbers naturally invites the user to say whether they want the assistant to be direct about errors and push back firmly versus soften the correction.

stages.evidence: “the numbers don’t line up with what I expected” / “sort out what’s actually wrong in it” / “if I need them to correct it”
stages.reason: The user explicitly wants the assistant to identify errors and help push back for correction, which strongly suggests they prefer firm correction rather than vague agreement when something is off.
